card.indications: Management of severe pain requiring an opioid when alternatives are inadequate. Parenteral morphine is also used in selected acute-care and anesthesia settings.
card.mechanism: Binds primarily to mu-opioid receptors in the CNS, altering perception and response to pain while producing sedation, respiratory depression, and decreased GI motility.
card.dosing: Dose is individualized by route, opioid tolerance, age, renal and hepatic function, and pain severity. Start lower in opioid-naive, older, frail, or renally impaired patients. Extended-release products are for around-the-clock pain and are not for rapid relief.
card.routeAdministration: Assess pain, sedation, respiratory rate, blood pressure, and oxygenation before and after administration. Give IV doses slowly. Swallow extended-release products whole unless the exact product permits opening; never crush or chew. Use an independent double-check for neuraxial routes.
card.contraindications: Significant respiratory depression, acute or severe bronchial asthma in an unmonitored setting, known or suspected GI obstruction including paralytic ileus, and hypersensitivity.
card.warnings: Boxed warnings include addiction, abuse, misuse, life-threatening respiratory depression, accidental ingestion, neonatal opioid withdrawal, and profound sedation or death with benzodiazepines or other CNS depressants.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Constipation, nausea, vomiting, drowsiness, dizziness, itching, sweating, urinary retention, and hypotension.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Apnea, severe respiratory depression, profound sedation, hypotension, anaphylaxis, ileus, seizures, adrenal insufficiency, serotonin syndrome, and dependence or withdrawal.
card.interactions: Benzodiazepines, alcohol, sedatives, antihistamines, and other opioids increase respiratory depression. MAO inhibitors and serotonergic drugs may increase serious reactions. Mixed agonist-antagonist opioids can precipitate withdrawal.
card.monitoring: Monitor pain relief, respiratory rate and depth, sedation score, oxygen saturation, blood pressure, pulse, bowel function, urinary retention, renal function, misuse risk, and naloxone availability.
card.patientTeaching: Take exactly as prescribed and do not share. Avoid alcohol and unapproved sedatives. Do not crush extended-release products. Report severe sleepiness or slow breathing and keep naloxone available when prescribed.
card.nutrition: Take oral doses with food if nausea occurs. Maintain fluids and fiber and use a bowel regimen unless contraindicated. Avoid alcohol.

card.indications: Emergency reversal of known or suspected opioid-induced respiratory and CNS depression and reversal of postoperative opioid effects.
card.mechanism: Competitively displaces opioids from mu, kappa, and delta receptors, rapidly reversing respiratory depression and sedation.
card.dosing: Dose depends on route, patient age, opioid exposure, and clinical response. Repeat doses may be needed every few minutes because naloxone duration is often shorter than the opioid involved. Continuous infusion may be required for long-acting opioids.
card.routeAdministration: Support airway and ventilation first. Administer promptly by the available route and repeat according to protocol. Call emergency services after community nasal use. Continue observation because recurrent respiratory depression can occur.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity is the only absolute contraindication in most emergency situations.
card.warnings: Abrupt reversal can precipitate acute opioid withdrawal with agitation, vomiting, hypertension, tachycardia, pulmonary edema, arrhythmias, or seizures. It does not replace airway support or emergency evaluation.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Nausea, vomiting, sweating, tremor, agitation, pain, tachycardia, and increased blood pressure.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Recurrent respiratory depression, pulmonary edema, severe withdrawal, ventricular arrhythmias, seizures, hypertensive crisis, and cardiac arrest in high-risk patients.
card.interactions: Reverses opioid analgesics and may precipitate withdrawal in opioid-dependent patients. It does not reverse nonopioid sedatives such as benzodiazepines.
card.monitoring: Monitor respiratory rate, oxygenation, ventilation, level of consciousness, pulse, blood pressure, ECG when indicated, withdrawal symptoms, pain recurrence, and need for repeat dosing.
card.patientTeaching: Teach family or caregivers how to recognize overdose, call emergency services, give naloxone, provide rescue breathing if trained, and repeat doses as directed. Stay with the person because symptoms can return.
card.nutrition: No food-related restrictions. After reversal, manage vomiting and aspiration risk before offering oral intake.

card.indications: Relief of pain and inflammation in osteoarthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, ankylosing spondylitis, gout, dysmenorrhea, and other acute musculoskeletal conditions.
card.mechanism: Inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes and reduces prostaglandin synthesis, decreasing pain, inflammation, and fever.
card.dosing: Dose depends on indication and formulation. Use the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration. Immediate-, delayed-, and extended-release products are not automatically interchangeable. Renal impairment and older age require caution.
card.routeAdministration: Give with food or milk if GI upset occurs and with a full glass of water. Do not crush delayed- or extended-release products. Avoid concurrent use with another NSAID unless specifically ordered.
card.contraindications: History of serious NSAID hypersensitivity, aspirin-related asthma or urticaria, and perioperative pain in CABG surgery.
card.warnings: Boxed warnings: increased risk of serious cardiovascular thrombotic events and serious or fatal GI bleeding, ulceration, and perforation. Other risks include renal injury, hypertension, heart failure, hyperkalemia, hepatotoxicity, anaphylaxis, and severe skin reactions.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Dyspepsia, nausea, abdominal pain, constipation, headache, dizziness, edema, and increased blood pressure.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: GI hemorrhage or perforation, myocardial infarction, stroke, acute kidney injury, severe hypertension, heart failure, hepatotoxicity, anaphylaxis, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, and toxic epidermal necrolysis.
card.interactions: Anticoagulants, antiplatelets, SSRIs, SNRIs, corticosteroids, alcohol, and other NSAIDs increase bleeding. ACE inhibitors, ARBs, and diuretics increase renal risk. Lithium and methotrexate concentrations may rise.
card.monitoring: Monitor pain and function, blood pressure, renal function, CBC with prolonged therapy, liver tests when indicated, edema, GI bleeding, and cardiovascular symptoms.
card.patientTeaching: Do not add ibuprofen, aspirin for pain, or another NSAID. Report black stools, vomiting blood, chest pain, shortness of breath, reduced urine, facial swelling, or severe rash.
card.nutrition: Take with food or milk if needed and maintain hydration unless restricted. Avoid excessive alcohol.

card.indications: Short-term IV treatment of selected hospitalized patients with acutely decompensated heart failure who have dyspnea at rest or with minimal activity.
card.mechanism: Binds natriuretic peptide receptors, increasing cyclic GMP and causing venous and arterial vasodilation, reduced filling pressures, and natriuresis.
card.dosing: Administered as a protocol-based IV loading dose followed by continuous infusion. Dose reduction or discontinuation is required for symptomatic hypotension. It is not used for routine outpatient therapy.
card.routeAdministration: Use an infusion pump and dedicated line when possible. Monitor blood pressure frequently during initiation and titration. Correct significant volume depletion before use and avoid mixing with incompatible drugs.
card.contraindications: Cardiogenic shock or systolic blood pressure below 90 mm Hg. Avoid when vasodilators are inappropriate, such as severe obstructive valvular disease or restrictive or constrictive conditions with preload dependence.
card.warnings: Can cause prolonged and severe hypotension. Renal function may worsen. It should not be used to replace appropriate diuresis or as intermittent scheduled therapy outside approved acute-care use.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Hypotension, headache, dizziness, nausea, abdominal discomfort, and infusion-related symptoms.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Severe or prolonged hypotension, syncope, renal deterioration, arrhythmias, myocardial ischemia from low perfusion, and hypersensitivity.
card.interactions: Other vasodilators, nitrates, antihypertensives, and diuretics can intensify hypotension. Concurrent nephrotoxic therapy may worsen renal function.
card.monitoring: Monitor blood pressure, heart rate, oxygenation, dyspnea, lung sounds, urine output, daily weight, renal function, electrolytes, volume status, and signs of hypoperfusion.
card.patientTeaching: Explain that continuous blood-pressure and urine monitoring are required. Report dizziness, faintness, chest pain, or worsening shortness of breath immediately.
card.nutrition: Follow the prescribed sodium and fluid restriction for heart failure. Oral intake may be limited during unstable symptoms.

card.indications: Prescription niacin may be used as an adjunct to diet for selected lipid disorders when benefits outweigh risks. Nutritional niacin is also used to prevent or treat deficiency.
card.mechanism: At lipid-lowering doses it decreases hepatic VLDL synthesis and reduces triglycerides and LDL while increasing HDL. It also has vitamin coenzyme functions at nutritional doses.
card.dosing: Lipid-lowering therapy starts low and is titrated gradually. Immediate- and extended-release products are not interchangeable milligram for milligram. Dose limits depend on formulation, liver function, and concurrent statin therapy.
card.routeAdministration: Give with food. Extended-release niacin is commonly taken at bedtime after a low-fat snack and swallowed whole. Avoid substituting over-the-counter or sustained-release products without prescriber approval.
card.contraindications: Active liver disease, unexplained persistent liver-enzyme elevation, active peptic ulcer disease, arterial bleeding, and hypersensitivity.
card.warnings: Can cause severe hepatotoxicity, hyperglycemia, hyperuricemia or gout, hypotension, myopathy with statins, bleeding, and atrial arrhythmias. Flushing is common but does not indicate allergy.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Flushing, warmth, itching, headache, dizziness, nausea, dyspepsia, and mild glucose elevation.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Hepatic failure, severe hyperglycemia, rhabdomyolysis, gout, serious hypotension, atrial fibrillation, severe GI bleeding, and anaphylaxis.
card.interactions: Statins increase myopathy risk. Alcohol and other hepatotoxic drugs increase liver injury. Antihypertensives increase hypotension. Diabetes and gout medications may require adjustment.
card.monitoring: Monitor lipid panel, liver tests, glucose or A1C, uric acid, blood pressure, muscle symptoms, and bleeding risk.
card.patientTeaching: Take exactly the prescribed formulation and do not switch brands or release forms. Flushing may lessen over time; avoid hot drinks or alcohol near the dose. Report severe muscle pain, dark urine, jaundice, or persistent abdominal pain.
card.nutrition: Take with food or a low-fat snack. Avoid alcohol and very hot beverages near the dose. Continue a lipid-lowering diet.
